---
title: "Supplementary: A software engineer's description of the model, for biologists"
author: "Steve Haigh"
date: today
format:
  pdf:
    pdf-engine: xelatex
    geometry: "margin=2.5cm"
    fontsize: 11pt
    mainfont: "STIX Two Text"
    monofont: "Menlo"
    colorlinks: true
    linkcolor: blue
    urlcolor: blue
    toc: false
    number-sections: true
    include-in-header: ../../pandoc-header.tex
  docx:
    toc: false
    number-sections: true
bibliography: ../references.bib
csl: ../harvard-leeds-beckett-university.csl
---

```{=html}
<!--
SKELETON. Planning notes in *[italic brackets]*. UNASSESSED but a valuable
artefact (and PhD onboarding doc). REGISTER WARNING: the audience is
biologists, not engineers. Motivate every technical choice BIOLOGICALLY;
each abstraction should answer "what biological thing does this represent?"
or it reads as code documentation. Keep code snippets minimal and explained.
Much of this can be drafted from CLAUDE.md + the codebase, then human-verified.
-->
```

## Purpose and audience

*[Who this is for and why it exists: a plain-language account of how a whole-cell model is built in software, aimed at biologists who will read, trust, or extend it. State the register explicitly.]*

## What a whole-cell model is, computationally

*[The core idea in one page: a set of biological processes that each modify a shared, mass-balanced inventory of molecules over time, with every protein counted. Contrast with a single ODE system. The biological pay-off: you can ask "what is the whole cell doing", not just "what is one pathway doing".]*

## Architecture: three layers

*[The framework / model / parameters split, with the biological motivation for each:]*

- **Framework (`wholecell/`)** — *[the reusable engine inherited from wcEcoli [@macklin2020]; analogy: the laboratory apparatus, independent of which cell you study].*
- **Model (`models/platelet/`)** — *[the platelet biology: processes and listeners].*
- **Reconstruction (`reconstruction/platelet/`)** — *[the parameters: copy numbers, rate constants, compartment volumes — the "cell's measured properties"].*

## The simulation loop

*[The 1-second timestep cycle in biologist's terms: processes declare what molecules they need → a partitioning step shares the limited pool → processes react → results merge → listeners record. Why discrete timesteps; why mass balance matters.]*

## Core abstractions, biologically

*[Table mapping each software abstraction to its biological meaning: Process = a mechanism (e.g. SERCA pumping); State = a molecular inventory in a compartment; Listener = an instrument recording a readout; Analysis = the figures. Compartment tags (`Ca[c]`, `Ca[dts]`, …).]*

## How the calcium biology maps to code

*[The `CalciumDynamics` process is a thin wrapper around the ODE right-hand side in `calcium_signalling.py`. Show how one mechanism (e.g. IP3R) becomes a flux term. Keep one short, annotated snippet. Explain the integrator (`scipy solve_ivp`, BDF) without jargon.]*

## Parameters as data

*[The kinetics-as-data refactor (#32): rate constants live in `reports/params/calcium-v0.5.toml`, species in `species-v0.5.tsv`, each value carrying a citation. Biological motivation: parameters are evidence, so they should be inspectable, sourced, and changeable without touching code.]*

## Reproducibility and engineering practice

*[Version control, automated tests, continuous integration, Docker, fixed seeds, `OPENBLAS_NUM_THREADS=1`. Frame as the computational analogue of a documented wet-lab protocol — this is the "repeatable methods" the marking descriptors reward (see `review-part-2.md` §9).]*

## Extending the model

*[How a new mechanism attaches as a process sharing the same Ca²⁺ state — the composition argument that distinguishes a whole-cell model from a single network. Note this is currently demonstrated for one pathway; adding a second sharing state is the decisive next step (cross-ref the feasibility supplement).]*

## References

::: {#refs}
:::
